text: Robotic Right Hemicolectomy on a Da Vinci X System. Presented by Dr. Somshekhar SP, Head of the Department and Consultant Surgical Oncologist, Manipal Comprehensive Cancer Center, Bangalore. A forty nine year old male with a ECOG of zero presented with loss of weight and generalized weakness since six months and change in the bowel habit since three months. On examination he had pallor. Abdominal examination was not significant. Hemoglobin on presentation was seven point two. On imaging, the ultrasound of the abdomen showed a mass lesion in the right iliac fossa arising from the ascending colon. CT scan showed a circumferential thickening of ascending colon with full thickness wall involvement of three point five centimeters and length of the involved segment around seven centimeters with luminal narrowing and fat stranding. There was no evidence of any ascites or metastasis. Serum CEA was one point six. Colonoscopy confirmed the findings with lumen compromised.

Biopsy revealed it to be a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma grade two with clinical stage as three B. Robotic right hemicolectomy with ileotransverse anastomosis was planned on the Da Vinci X system. Principles of a right hemicolectomy include thorough inspection of the abdomen and assessment of operability, mobilization of hepatic flexure of colon, identification of vascular pedicles and ligation, mobilization of colon from right lateral parietal wall identification and preservation of right ureter, careful dissection of the duodenum, transaction of terminal ileum and transverse colon with adequate margins, and ileotransverse anastomosis. The main vascular pedicles in terms of a right hemicolectomy are right branch of middle colic artery, right colic artery and the ileocolic artery which are the branches of superior mesentric artery.

The patient is positioned supine for robotic docking. The camera port is placed in the subumbilical line to the left, with robotic instrument ports R-I in the supraumbilical region below the costal arch, R-II in the suprapubic region to the left, and R-III in the right iliac fossa. The assistant port is triangulated between R-I and R-II. The instrument cart is positioned at the patient's right shoulder, while the assistant and scrub nurse are located to the left.

The surgeon uses hot shears in R-I, a vessel sealer in R-II, and a tip-up prograsp in R-III to begin the dissection. The hepatic flexure of the colon is visualized and mobilized first. The dissection proceeds towards the right lateral parietal wall, revealing the underlying duodenum. Careful dissection is performed around the duodenum using monopolar hot shears to avoid iatrogenic injury.

The surgeon visualizes the right branch of the middle colic vein, with the duodenum and head of the pancreas clearly visible below. Dissection of the greater omentum from the mesocolon is underway, revealing the branching pattern of the middle colic vein.

The surgeon separates the cecum from the white line of Toldt in the right iliac fossa, identifying the right-sided gonadal vessels and ureter. The dissection proceeds cranially, mobilizing the terminal ileum. The cecum and appendix are lifted, and the ileocolic pedicle is ligated using a vessel sealer. The right colic pedicle is also ligated, followed by the right branch of the middle colic pedicle. The transverse colon is lifted, and the splenic flexure is mobilized to reduce tension on the anastomosis, completing the mobilization from the terminal ileum to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon completes the intracorporeal dissection and mobilization, then makes a right-sided lower transverse abdominal incision. A wound guard is used to extract the specimen, ensuring careful handling to avoid direct contact with the tumor. The ileocolic anastomosis is performed as a side-to-side anastomosis using a stapler.

The total operative time in this case was two hundred and ten minutes with console time of sixty five minutes. Blood loss was around ninety ml and patient recovered uneventfully in the post operative period.

The final histopathology report was adenocarcinoma low-grade moderately differentiated of a size of ten by six by two centimeters. Margins were all uninvolved. Tumor invaded the subserosal adipose tissue but not extended to the serosal surface. There was no lymphovascular invasion. Lymph nodes were uninvolved. and around twenty-five lymph nodes were extracted. MSI being high, the patient had a stage IIa right-sided colonic malignancy, i.e. PT-N-M-M. Hence, patient was kept on observation and is on a regular follow-up. Thank you.
